description_short_en: Compass Box pioneered the category of premium blended and blended-grain Scotch whisky, releasing Hedonism in 2000 as the world's first commercial blended grain Scotch whisky, directly challenging the single-malt orthodoxy. The house has…
description_full_en: Founded 2000 by John Glaser. Owner: Cælum Capital Limited (majority stake acquired 2022); John Glaser retains a minority ownership stake. Region: Scotland (blending house based in Richmond/London; sources malt and grain whiskies from distilleries across Islay, Speyside, the Highlands, and the Lowlands). Classification: Blended Scotch Whisky / Blended Malt Scotch Whisky / Blended Grain Scotch Whisky — varies by expression. No geographic GI appellation; regulated under Scotch Whisky Regulations 2009.. Style: Compass Box operates as a boutique blending house — not a distillery — sourcing high-quality malt and grain casks from across Scotland and assembling them with a winemaker's precision, prioritising flavour architecture over heritage convention. Their house style spans a wide spectrum: from the silky, vanilla-rich grain-forward Hedonism, to the heavily peated Peat Monster (Caol Ila and Laphroaig dominant), to the French-oak-spiced Spice Tree — all unified by meticulous cask selection, frequent use of custom-toasted French oak, and a deliberate rejection of mass-market blending norms. Expressions are typically bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill-filtered, and without added colouring, giving them more texture and vibrancy than most commercial blends. Site: No owned distillery. Compass Box operates a blending room in Richmond, London. Component whiskies are sourced from named Scottish distilleries including Caol Ila, Laphroaig, Clynelish, Linkwood, Benrinnes, Balmenach, Glen Elgin, Talisker, and Cameronbridge (primary grain source for Hedonism), among others. Compass Box holds its own maturing stocks in bonded warehouses in Scotland and commissions bespoke custom casks with specified toast levels. Vinification: No distillation performed by Compass Box. Component whiskies are distilled at source distilleries under standard Scotch Whisky Regulations (pot still for malts, column still for grains). Compass Box selects specific parcels/casks at the distillery level, then blends and re-matures in London-specified casks. Aging: Primary maturation at source distilleries in ex-bourbon (first-fill and refill American oak hogsheads/barrels), ex-Oloroso sherry casks, and refill casks. Secondary/additional maturation in Compass Box's own custom-made casks featuring heavily toasted French oak heads (replacing the inner staves banned by the SWA in 2006). Specific durations vary by expression; Hedonism averages approximately 23 years across components. Peat Monster components aged in refill hogsheads and ex-bourbon barrels. Bottling: Typically bottled at 46% ABV. Non-chill-filtered. No added colouring (natural colour). Select limited editions bottled at cask strength (e.g. Peat Monster Cask Strength 20th Anniversary). Standard expressions are not cask strength. Blend/recipe: Varies by expression. Orchard House (Blended Malt): Linkwood ~39%, Clynelish ~29%, Benrinnes ~20%, plus other Highland malts; aged in ex-bourbon, ex-Oloroso sherry, and French oak. Hedonism (Blended Grain): primarily Cameronbridge grain whisky, with multiple grain distillery parcels (3–7 single grains per release); first-fill and antique sherry casks. Peat Monster (Blended Malt): Caol Ila and Laphroaig as majority components (heavily peated Islay), plus medium-peated Highland malts; refill hogsheads and ex-bourbon. Flaming Heart (Blended Malt, limited): multi-distillery including Laphroaig, Glen Elgin, Balmenach, Caol Ila, Talisker, and a custom French oak Highland malt blend. Must know: Compass Box pioneered the category of premium blended and blended-grain Scotch whisky, releasing Hedonism in 2000 as the world's first commercial blended grain Scotch whisky, directly challenging the single-malt orthodoxy. The house has twice clashed with the Scotch Whisky Association — first over the use of French oak inner staves in The Spice Tree (2005–2006), and again in 2015 over full label transparency of component whiskies — making it the most publicly activist producer in the modern Scotch industry.

description_short_en: Dassai is the only major Japanese brewery to produce exclusively Junmai Daiginjo across its entire commercial range — a category commitment considered extraordinary even in Japan — and was the first sake brewery in Japan to deploy a…
description_full_en: Founded 1948 (as Asahi Shuzo Co., Ltd.; Sakurai family took over the original brewery site — established in the 1700s — during the Meiji era late 1800s; Dassai brand launched 1990 by Hiroshi Sakurai). Owner: Asahi Shuzo Co., Ltd. — privately held by the Sakurai family (Hiroshi Sakurai, Chairman; Kazuhiro Sakurai, President & CEO, 4th generation). Region: Shuto-cho, Iwakuni City, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Southern Honshu, Japan. Classification: Junmai Daiginjo (highest premium grade of sake; no added distilled alcohol; rice polished to ≤50% of original grain size). Full range: Dassai 45 (45% seimaibuai), Dassai 39 (39%), Dassai 23 (23%), Dassai Beyond (undisclosed ultra-low ratio).. Style: Dassai produces exclusively Junmai Daiginjo — an almost unheard-of single-tier commitment in the sake world — using only Yamada Nishiki rice across its entire range, yielding a house style of refined, fruit-forward elegance (ripe melon, pear, lychee, apple) with silky texture and clean, lingering finish. Unlike most breweries that blend grades or use seasonal toji (master brewers), Dassai employs year-round permanent staff and runs parallel brewing teams that experiment with fermentation variables, combining artisan handcraft with rigorous data analysis to achieve consistent premium quality at scale. Site: Main brewery: a 12-story reinforced concrete facility (~127,000 sq ft total floor space) in the mountains of Shuto-cho, Iwakuni City, Yamaguchi Prefecture — approximately 30 minutes by car from central Iwakuni, deep in a lush mountain valley. Iwakuni is historically renowned for its soft, pure water (classified as 'Seiryu' — clear stream water), which is a key terroir element. The brewery has made significant investment in water purification. Specific soil/elevation data for the brewery site not publicly documented. Vinification: Batch-by-batch fermentation in small individual tanks; handmade koji (Aspergillus oryzae) applied manually — human hands essential, not automated; rice washed in 10 kg units in running water and soaked ~15 minutes; fermentation temperature carefully controlled throughout moromi (main mash) to manage yeast activity and aroma development; two parallel brewing teams experiment with differing water absorption rates and fermentation temperatures and compare results. No toji system — permanent year-round employed staff. Aging: Specific aging vessel type and duration not publicly disclosed by the brewery. Sake is not wood-aged (standard for the category). Low-temperature storage standard for Junmai Daiginjo. Bottling: Pasteurization via patented 'Hayata method' (Patent 2013-008455): bottled at low temperature (<5°C) then hot-water-shower pasteurized, immediately rapid-cooled to preserve freshness; commercialized 2016. Dassai is also the first sake brewery in Japan to use a centrifuge (pioneered commercially c. 2000) to separate sake from mash without pressure, preserving delicate aroma and texture. Typical ABV ~16% (standard Junmai Daiginjo range); cask-strength or unfiltered expressions not standard across the range. Blend/recipe: 100% Yamada Nishiki sake rice ('king of sake rice') across all expressions. No added distilled alcohol (Junmai). Rice polishing ratios by SKU: Dassai 45 = 45% seimaibuai (55% milled away); Dassai 39 = 39% (61% milled away); Dassai 23 = 23% (77% milled away, requiring ~168 hours of continuous milling); Dassai Beyond = undisclosed ultra-low ratio (reportedly a few thousand bottles annually). Water: soft mountain water from Iwakuni, purified on-site. Must know: Dassai is the only major Japanese brewery to produce exclusively Junmai Daiginjo across its entire commercial range — a category commitment considered extraordinary even in Japan — and was the first sake brewery in Japan to deploy a centrifuge for commercial pressing, separating sake from mash without pressure to preserve maximum aroma integrity. The Dassai 23, whose 168-hour polishing process was extended a final 24 hours after a competitor reached 24% seimaibuai, effectively set the modern benchmark for ultra-polished premium sake and catalysed the global sake boom.

parent_region: Highland Perthshire, Scotland (heart distillery: Aberfeldy, Perthshire Highlands); blending and bottling at Glasgow
description_short_en: Dewar's pioneered the 'Double Aging' (marrying) process — blending first, then re-casking the finished blend for a minimum six months — a technique now central to the entire portfolio and widely credited as the origin of the modern…
description_full_en: Founded 1846 by John Dewar (born 1805, Dull, Perthshire). Owner: Bacardi Limited (acquired 1998; privately held, Bermuda). Region: Highland Perthshire, Scotland (heart distillery: Aberfeldy, Perthshire Highlands); blending and bottling at Glasgow. Classification: Blended Scotch Whisky (no geographic sub-appellation classification; not a single malt or single grain). Style: Dewar's is defined by its proprietary 'Double Aging' (or 'marrying') process: component whiskies are individually matured, blended, then returned to oak casks for a further minimum six months before bottling, producing a rounder, more integrated character than most blended Scotch at equivalent price points. The house flavour profile centres on heather honey, vanilla, soft fruit, and a wisp of smoke — a profile anchored by Aberfeldy single malt as the 'heart' of every blend. The prestige Double Double Collection extends this to a four-stage aging process (single malts and grains aged separately, blended by type, re-casked, combined as a blend, rested again, then finished in expressive casks), the first such process in the whisky industry. Site: Aberfeldy Distillery, eastern outskirts of Aberfeldy, Highland Perthshire (founded 1896, production from 1898; designed by architect Charles Doig). Water source: the Pitilie Burn, a natural artesian spring unique to Aberfeldy, running alongside the distillery into the River Tay. Terrain: rolling Perthshire hills, southern bank of the upper Tay. Four copper pot stills (two wash stills at ~16,500 L, two spirit stills at ~15,000 L); larch washbacks with extra-long fermentation. New-make spirit transported ~70 miles by tanker to Dewar's maturation and bottling plant in Glasgow. John Dewar & Sons also owns four additional malt distilleries used in the blend: Aultmore (Speyside), Craigellachie (Speyside, worm-tub condensers), Royal Brackla (Northern Highlands), and Macduff (Highlands). Vinification: Traditional Scotch whisky production: malting (Aberfeldy uses unpeated Optic-variety Scottish barley, malted by Simpsons of Berwick-upon-Tweed), mashing in a 6.3-tonne stainless steel full-Lauter mash tun, fermentation in larch washbacks (extra-long fermentation to develop honeyed character), double distillation in medium-height onion-shaped copper pot stills. First distillation ~4 hours (22–26% ABV); second distillation ~8 hours (new make at 68–72% ABV). Blending of up to 30 (some sources cite up to 40) single malt and grain whiskies conducted by Master Blender Stephanie Macleod primarily by nosing at the Glasgow blending facility. Aging: Primary maturation predominantly in American oak ex-bourbon casks; post-blend 'marrying' period of minimum six months in oak casks (Double Aging). The 12 Year Old receives a first-fill bourbon cask finish. The Double Double Collection (21, 27, 32 years+) undergoes four-stage aging with final finishes in Oloroso, Pedro Ximénez, or Madeira casks. Cask finishes series (8 Year Old range) includes ex-rum, ex-mezcal, ex-port, Mizunara oak, and Calvados casks. Bottling: Standard expressions bottled at 40% ABV; cask-strength or unfiltered expressions not confirmed for core range. Chill-filtration status not publicly specified for standard expressions. Bottling at Glasgow plant. Blend/recipe: Up to 30 single malt and grain whiskies (some sources cite up to 40 single malts alone for certain expressions); Aberfeldy single malt is the largest-percentage and 'heart' component, contributing heather honey and oak. Supporting malts from Craigellachie (Speyside sweetness, worm-tub character), Royal Brackla (delicate, fruity), Aultmore (Speyside), and Macduff (Highland), plus grain whisky from external Scottish partners. Exact varietal percentages and grain-to-malt ratio are proprietary and not publicly disclosed. Must know: Dewar's pioneered the 'Double Aging' (marrying) process — blending first, then re-casking the finished blend for a minimum six months — a technique now central to the entire portfolio and widely credited as the origin of the modern 'marrying' practice in Scotch blending. The brand also claims credit for popularising the Scotch Highball in the United States in the 1890s via Tommy Dewar, and was the first blended Scotch to receive a Royal Warrant from Queen Victoria.

parent_region: Champagne AOC, France — grapes drawn from Grand Cru and Premier Cru villages across Montagne de Reims, Côte des Blancs, and Vallée de la Marne, anchored by the historic Premier Cru plots at Abbaye d'Hautvillers near Épernay.
description_short_en: Dom Pérignon is the world's original prestige cuvée and the only major Champagne house that produces exclusively vintage wine — if a harvest does not meet standards, no Dom Pérignon is made that year. Its Plénitude system, pioneered by…
description_full_en: Founded 1921 (first vintage); brand transferred to Moët & Chandon in 1927 by Champagne Mercier; first commercial release 1936. Named after Dom Pierre Pérignon (1638–1715), Benedictine monk and cellarmaster at Abbaye d'Hautvillers from 1668.. Owner: LVMH (Moët Hennessy Louis Vuitton) via subsidiary Moët & Chandon; Dom Pérignon operates as a separate prestige-cuvée brand within the group.. Region: Champagne AOC, France — grapes drawn from Grand Cru and Premier Cru villages across Montagne de Reims, Côte des Blancs, and Vallée de la Marne, anchored by the historic Premier Cru plots at Abbaye d'Hautvillers near Épernay.. Classification: Champagne AOC — Prestige Cuvée (tête de cuvée); 100% vintage-declared; no non-vintage release ever produced.. Style: Dom Pérignon is defined by an intensely reductive winemaking philosophy: every stage of production minimises oxygen contact, producing a signature smoky, mineral-driven aromatic alongside pristine citrus and stone fruit. Unlike most grandes marques that blend multiple years, Dom Pérignon is exclusively vintage and is only declared in exceptional harvests, meaning no wine is released in weaker years. The house's unique Plénitude (P1/P2/P3) system releases the same vintage at three distinct maturation windows — approximately 8–9, 15, and 30–40 years — each representing a different peak of expression rather than a linear ageing curve. Site: Grapes sourced from a fixed core of nine crus: Chardonnay from Chouilly, Cramant, Avize, and Le Mesnil-sur-Oger (Côte des Blancs); Pinot Noir from Aÿ, Bouzy, Mailly-Champagne, Verzenay (Montagne de Reims), and Hautvillers (Vallée de la Marne, Premier Cru). Dom Pérignon has access to approximately 1,200 hectares of vines, including 600 hectares of Grand Cru and Premier Cru classified land. Terroir is predominantly deep chalk and limestone — chalk acts as a moisture reservoir, ensuring vine hydration in dry periods. Hautvillers sits on moderately steep south-facing slopes above the Marne valley, combining good drainage with optimal sun exposure. Vinification: Hand-harvested; whole-cluster pressing using traditional Coquard presses to minimise skin contact and extract clear, low-tannin juice. Chardonnay and Pinot Noir vinified separately in stainless steel tanks to preserve varietal purity and freshness. Malolactic fermentation is conducted. Chef de Cave Vincent Chaperon (since 2019) uses two distinct yeast strains for primary fermentation and two further strains for the prise de mousse (secondary in-bottle fermentation), with yeast preparation considered a key driver of the wine's long-term ageing capacity. Reductive handling throughout — oxygen exposure minimised at every step. Aging: Extended lees ageing in bottle, undisgorged, in chalk cellars. P1 (standard vintage): minimum 8–9 years on lees. P2 (Second Plénitude): minimum 15 years on lees. P3 (Third Plénitude): 30–40 years on lees, with no fewer than 20 years contact. No oak used; all ageing is reductive, in bottle on lees. Not every vintage qualifies for P2 or P3 release. Bottling: Brut style; dosage typically 4.5–5 g/L (vintage-dependent). Chill-filtered status not officially disclosed. ABV approximately 12.5%. Not cask strength. Standard release is the P1 vintage; P2 and P3 are disgorged at the chef de cave's discretion when a 'window of plenitude' is identified. Blend/recipe: Always 100% Pinot Noir and Chardonnay — no Pinot Meunier. Approximate target is 50/50, but actual split varies by vintage: e.g. 2015 = 51% Pinot Noir / 49% Chardonnay; 2013 = 51% Pinot Noir / 49% Chardonnay; 1982 reached ~60% Chardonnay; 1969 reached ~60% Pinot Noir. The goal is seamless harmony between the two varieties, not a fixed formula. Must know: Dom Pérignon is the world's original prestige cuvée and the only major Champagne house that produces exclusively vintage wine — if a harvest does not meet standards, no Dom Pérignon is made that year. Its Plénitude system, pioneered by former chef de cave Richard Geoffroy and continued by Vincent Chaperon, is unique in Champagne: the same vintage is held undisgorged on lees and released at three separate maturation peaks (P1, P2, P3), meaning a collector can taste the identical harvest at radically different stages of evolution — a concept with no true parallel among other grandes marques.

parent_region: Chassagne-Montrachet AOC, Côte de Beaune, Burgundy, France (with additional holdings in Puligny-Montrachet, Saint-Aubin, Santenay, and Maranges)
description_short_en: Domaine Philippe Colin is one of only a handful of producers to vinify Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Demoiselles (part of Le Cailleret, adjacent to Montrachet), releasing a mere ~300 bottles per year — a near-impossible allocation for any…
description_full_en: Founded 2004 by Philippe Colin (after partition of the family estate Domaine Michel Colin-Déléger; family roots in Chassagne-Montrachet trace to the 1850s). Owner: Independent family domaine — Philippe Colin (currently transitioning majority of vineyards to son Simon Colin from 2021 vintage onward). Region: Chassagne-Montrachet AOC, Côte de Beaune, Burgundy, France (with additional holdings in Puligny-Montrachet, Saint-Aubin, Santenay, and Maranges). Classification: AOC Burgundy — range spans Bourgogne régionale, Village (Chassagne-Montrachet, Puligny-Montrachet, Saint-Aubin, Santenay), Premier Cru (Les Chenevottes, Les Vergers, Les Chaumées Clos Saint-Abdon, En Remilly, Puligny-Montrachet Les Demoiselles, Saint-Aubin Les Combes), and Grand Cru (Chevalier-Montrachet). Style: Philippe Colin is defined by elegance and precision over weight and power — a deliberate counter-position to the richer, more opulent style of some Côte de Beaune peers. Whites show vibrant acidity, crystalline minerality, and restrained oak influence, with fermentation starting in stainless steel to preserve Chardonnay's floral character before finishing in barrel. Reds are lithe and perfumed, made with full destemming and moderate extraction to produce fine-tannin, energy-driven Pinot Noir rather than extracted, oak-heavy styles. Site: Estate currently covers approximately 5 hectares (Philippe's retained share post-2021 handover to Simon), with the broader Colin family footprint at ~11–13 ha across multiple appellations. Village Chassagne-Montrachet vines (35–40 years old) are planted in limestone and calcareous clay soils with a high clay content on an eastern aspect, just below the Premier Cru slopes. Les Chaumées Clos Saint-Abdon sits at the highest elevation of the northern Chassagne slope bordering Saint-Aubin, with shallow soils of higher limestone-to-clay ratio imparting bracing minerality. Chevalier-Montrachet Grand Cru (vines planted 1955 and 1978) features shallow, well-drained limestone and calcareous clay on steeply sloping inclines in Puligny-Montrachet. Les Demoiselles (part of Le Cailleret, Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru) has light, well-drained mixed clay soils despite appearing heavy. Vineyards are farmed under lutte raisonnée (reasoned/sustainable viticulture) with rigorous plowing and minimal chemical use; no biodynamic certification. Vinification: Whites: manual harvest, direct pneumatic pressing, alcoholic fermentation begins in thermoregulated stainless steel tanks at low temperature (to preserve floral Chardonnay character), then transferred to barrel to complete fermentation and undergo malolactic fermentation. Reds: manual harvest with vibrating sorting table, full destemming, cold pre-fermentation maceration, then fermentation in tank with moderate extraction. Aging: Whites: French oak barrels of 228 L, 450 L, and 500 L from 5 different cooperages (25% new oak at village level), aged 12–18 months on fine lees with periodic stirring, then 3–6 months additional rest in stainless steel vat before bottling. Reds: 500 L barrels exclusively, 10–12 months, followed by 3–6 months in stainless steel vat. Bottling: Light filtration before bottling. Whites from Chassagne village level through Chevalier-Montrachet Grand Cru receive a distinctive technical wax seal (2–3 mm above the cork), applied since 2011. ABV typically ~13% for village whites. No cask-strength or unfiltered declarations confirmed. Blend/recipe: Whites: 100% Chardonnay across all appellations. Village Chassagne-Montrachet is a blend of up to 15 separate parcels (lieux-dits including Voillenot Dessous, Concis des Champs, Les Charrières, Fontaine Sot), vinified separately and blended pre-bottling to balance the lighter, more mineral Puligny-side parcels with the more powerful Santenay-side parcels. Reds: 100% Pinot Noir from Chassagne-Montrachet and Santenay parcels. Must know: Domaine Philippe Colin is one of only a handful of producers to vinify Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Demoiselles (part of Le Cailleret, adjacent to Montrachet), releasing a mere ~300 bottles per year — a near-impossible allocation for any retailer. The domaine also broke from the Burgundy norm by pioneering a technical wax capsule seal on all whites from village level upward (since 2011), and by deliberately starting white fermentation in stainless steel before moving to barrel — an unusual hybrid approach that preserves Chardonnay's floral aromatics while still gaining textural complexity from oak.

parent_region: Vosne-Romanée, Côte de Nuits, Burgundy, France — with parcels extending into Flagey-Échézeaux and northern Nuits-Saint-Georges
description_short_en: From 1990 to 2010 the entire domaine's production was sold in bulk to négociants Louis Jadot and Joseph Drouhin — meaning the estate's current reputation was built entirely by 20-year-old Maxime Cheurlin from his very first vintage in…
description_full_en: Founded Early 20th century by Georges Noëllat (exact year unknown); domaine passed to current generation in 2010 under Maxime Cheurlin. Owner: Cheurlin-Noëllat family (independent); managed by Maxime Cheurlin, grandson of Marie-Thérèse Noëllat. Region: Vosne-Romanée, Côte de Nuits, Burgundy, France — with parcels extending into Flagey-Échézeaux and northern Nuits-Saint-Georges. Classification: Multiple AOC levels: Grands Crus (Échézeaux, Grands Échézeaux); Premiers Crus across Vosne-Romanée (Les Beaux Monts, Les Chaumes, Les Petits Monts), Nuits-Saint-Georges (Aux Boudots, Aux Cras), Chambolle-Musigny (Les Feusselottes); Village and Bourgogne Rouge. 100% Pinot Noir red wines.. Style: Maxime Cheurlin pursues an 'infusion' rather than extraction philosophy — punch-downs are rare and gentle, yielding wines of lacy, ethereal elegance rather than the power-driven style common among Côte de Nuits peers. The house is distinguished by extremely low yields (averaging ~25 hl/ha), a deliberate cooling of the cellar to delay malolactic fermentation for added precision and complexity, and a portfolio anchored in pre-WWII old vines that deliver uncommon depth for their appellation level. Site: 5.5 hectares spanning Vosne-Romanée and northern Nuits-Saint-Georges, with the domaine cellar located at 1 Rue des Chaumes, Vosne-Romanée, next door to Sylvain Cathiard. Soils are classic Côte de Nuits clay-limestone (argilo-calcaire), with the limestone providing excellent drainage and aromatic lift and the clay contributing concentration. Key sites include Les Beaux Monts 1er Cru (old vines >80 years), Échézeaux and Grands Échézeaux Grand Cru (Flagey-Échézeaux). Some vines are over 100 years old. Viticulture follows lutte raisonnée (no insecticides or chemical fertilisers); all work is done by hand. Vinification: Grapes are hand-harvested and meticulously sorted. Typically 100% destemmed, with occasional whole-cluster inclusion up to ~30% in select vintages. Cold pre-fermentation maceration for several days; fermentation with indigenous (native) yeasts only. Maxime describes his approach as 'infusion' rather than extraction — punch-downs are rare and very gentle. Aging: French oak barrels, 14–20 months. New oak proportion varies by appellation and vintage: approximately 30% for village-level wines up to 100% for Grand Cru. Cellar is actively cooled to delay and slow malolactic fermentation, a technique adopted from around the 2017 vintage onward. Bottling: Unfined and unfiltered. ABV not specified in sources; standard Burgundy range (~12.5–13.5% ABV). Not cask strength. Blend/recipe: 100% Pinot Noir across all cuvées. No blending across appellations; each wine is a single-climat, single-varietal expression. Village Vosne-Romanée may blend multiple lieu-dits (e.g., Champ Perdrix, Haute Maizières, Pré de la Folie). Must know: From 1990 to 2010 the entire domaine's production was sold in bulk to négociants Louis Jadot and Joseph Drouhin — meaning the estate's current reputation was built entirely by 20-year-old Maxime Cheurlin from his very first vintage in 2010. Cheurlin is a blood relative of the Jayer family and a cousin of Emmanuel Rouget's sons, placing him at the epicentre of Vosne-Romanée's most celebrated winemaking lineage; his deliberate use of cellar cooling to delay malolactic fermentation is an unusual technical choice that critics (notably Allen Meadows/Burghound) credit with adding precision and complexity to wines already praised for their 'delicate' terroir expression.

description_short_en: Domaine Leflaive is the reference benchmark for biodynamic white Burgundy at Grand Cru level — Anne-Claude Leflaive's full conversion of the estate by 1997 was considered radical at the time and is now credited with legitimising…
description_full_en: Founded 1717 by Claude Leflaive (family roots); modern domaine consolidated 1905–1930 by Joseph Leflaive (1870–1953). Owner: Leflaive family (independent; currently led by Brice de La Morandière, 4th generation, since 2015). Region: Puligny-Montrachet AOC, Côte de Beaune, Burgundy, France. Classification: Burgundy AOC hierarchy: Grand Cru (Montrachet, Chevalier-Montrachet, Bâtard-Montrachet, Bienvenues-Bâtard-Montrachet — 4.8 ha total); Premier Cru Puligny-Montrachet (Les Pucelles, Le Clavoillon, Les Folatières, Les Combettes — 10.8 ha total); Village Puligny-Montrachet; Bourgogne Blanc. Also one red: Blagny Sous le Dos d'Âne Premier Cru (Pinot Noir).. Style: Leflaive wines are defined by crystalline mineral precision, tightly coiled acidity, and exceptional longevity rather than overt richness or oak influence — a style that prioritises terroir transparency above all. Compared to peers such as Domaine Ramonet or Domaine Coche-Dury, Leflaive wines tend toward greater tension and restraint in youth, unfurling into layered, resonant expressions only with extended cellaring. The biodynamic conversion (complete by 1996–1997) is widely credited with amplifying the vibrancy and site-specificity that distinguishes each cuvée. Site: 24 ha in Puligny-Montrachet, situated ~11 km south of Beaune on the mid-slopes of the Côte de Beaune. Soils are calcareous clay (argilo-calcaire) with varying clay/limestone ratios by site, plus trace elements of iron, magnesium, and boron; upper slopes trend toward purer limestone. Key sites: Le Clavoillon (4.79 ha, single parcel, argilo-calcaire, Leflaive owns >85%); Les Pucelles (2.8 ha, fine clay-limestone, east-facing, borders Bienvenues-Bâtard-Montrachet); Les Folatières (1.26 ha, 3 parcels, more calcareous); Les Combettes (0.73 ha, edge of Meursault). Village-level parcels sit at ~215 m elevation with southeast exposure. Grand Cru and select Premier Cru plots are ploughed by horse to minimise soil compaction. Since 2004, additional holdings in Mâcon-Verzé and Solutré-Pouilly (Mâconnais), ~17–20 ha. Vinification: Manual harvest with parcel-by-parcel ripeness monitoring and sorting table. Long, gentle pneumatic pressing followed by 24-hour static settling (débourbage). Must racked directly into oak casks for alcoholic fermentation using indigenous (native) yeasts only. Light bâtonnage practiced between end of alcoholic fermentation and onset of malolactic fermentation. Wines spend first winter in barrel, second winter in stainless steel tank. Aging: 12 months in French oak casks (Vosges and Allier; ratio varies by cuvée — typically maxi 1/3 Vosges, mini 2/3 Allier), followed by 6 months in stainless steel tank prior to bottling. New oak percentage varies by appellation level: ~18–22% new oak for village and Premier Cru; up to ~25% for Grand Cru. Total élevage approximately 18 months. Bottling: Homeopathic (minimal) fining and very light filtration only if necessary; effectively near-unfiltered in practice. Bottled in spring of the second year. Conventional ABV (typically ~13%). Not cask strength. Blend/recipe: 100% Chardonnay across all white cuvées. No blending of appellations. Village Puligny-Montrachet is an assemblage of 7 parcels (4.64 ha total). Each Premier Cru and Grand Cru is vinified and bottled separately by climat. The sole red (Blagny 1er Cru) is 100% Pinot Noir. Must know: Domaine Leflaive is the reference benchmark for biodynamic white Burgundy at Grand Cru level — Anne-Claude Leflaive's full conversion of the estate by 1997 was considered radical at the time and is now credited with legitimising biodynamics across the entire Côte d'Or. Unusually, the domaine owns more than 85% of the Le Clavoillon Premier Cru climat, effectively controlling its identity, and its Les Pucelles Premier Cru competed in the 1976 Judgment of Paris.

parent_region: Meursault, Côte de Beaune, Burgundy, France — with sourced parcels spanning Côte de Beaune and Côte de Nuits appellations including Pommard, Volnay, Puligny-Montrachet, Corton-Charlemagne, and Le Montrachet
description_short_en: Pierre-Vincent Girardin (born c.1996) is the 13th generation of a Burgundian winemaking family and launched his domaine at age 18–19 with his debut 2017 vintage — an almost unprecedented achievement in a region where reputation typically…
description_full_en: Founded 2016 (first vintage 2017) by Pierre-Vincent Girardin. Owner: Independent; Pierre-Vincent Girardin (family-owned). Region: Meursault, Côte de Beaune, Burgundy, France — with sourced parcels spanning Côte de Beaune and Côte de Nuits appellations including Pommard, Volnay, Puligny-Montrachet, Corton-Charlemagne, and Le Montrachet. Classification: AOC Burgundy — range spans Bourgogne régionale through Premier Cru (e.g. Meursault Les Perrières 1er Cru, Pommard Les Épenots 1er Cru, Volnay Clos des Chênes 1er Cru) and Grand Cru (Le Montrachet, Bâtard-Montrachet, Chevalier-Montrachet, Corton-Charlemagne, Griotte-Chambertin, Clos de Vougeot). No official 1855-style classification applies in Burgundy.. Style: Pierre-Vincent Girardin prioritises mineral purity and terroir transparency over richness or oak influence — his whites are described as 'crunchy,' electric, and tightly wound, with pronounced acidity and salinity, while his reds aim for silkiness and finesse rather than extraction. Compared to peers such as Coche-Dury or Pierre-Yves Colin-Morey, his approach is notably low-interventionist: no bâtonnage, minimal sulphur, and bottling unfined and unfiltered, resulting in wines of crystalline freshness and site-specific character. Site: Estate-owned parcels total approximately 4.8–6.5 ha centred on Meursault, Pommard, and Volnay; soils are predominantly Jurassic marls and marly limestones typical of the Côte de Beaune mid-slope, at elevations around 260 m with east-to-south exposures. Vines are trained high (1.60–1.70 m canopy) to optimise photosynthesis and preserve freshness. Additional fruit is sourced via long-term contracts with like-minded organic growers across the Côte d'Or, including rare parcels of Le Montrachet, Chambertin, Chevalier-Montrachet, and Clos de Vougeot. A separate Jura project (6+ ha, Rotalier and Vincelles) was launched from 2021, on limestone scree soils at 230–300 m altitude. Vinification: Grapes hand-harvested and carefully sorted; whites fermented with indigenous yeasts in custom 456-litre oak barrels (François Frères / Seguin-Moreau cooperages) with no bâtonnage and minimal sulphur; reds vinified with whole-cluster inclusion (100% whole-grape from 2021), long gentle press, little to no pigeage. Cellar maintained cold for purity of juice. Bottling timed to the lunar calendar. Aging: Whites: 12 months in 456-litre barrels (30% new oak for village and above), then 6 months in stainless-steel tank before bottling. Reds: 12 months in standard 228-litre barrels (15 months for grands crus), then 3 months in tank. New oak kept low to preserve freshness. Bottling: Unfined and unfiltered across the range; no chill-filtration stated; bottled at natural ABV (not cask strength); bottling dates follow the lunar calendar. Blend/recipe: 100% Chardonnay for all white cuvées; 100% Pinot Noir for all red cuvées — single-varietal, single-appellation, single-climat bottlings throughout. Estate vineyard split is approximately 40% Chardonnay / 60% Pinot Noir by planted area, though white wine production dominates the prestige range. Average production per cuvée is approximately 1,400 bottles. Must know: Pierre-Vincent Girardin (born c.1996) is the 13th generation of a Burgundian winemaking family and launched his domaine at age 18–19 with his debut 2017 vintage — an almost unprecedented achievement in a region where reputation typically takes decades to build. His use of custom oversized 456-litre barrels (vs. the Burgundy-standard 228-litre pièce) to reduce wood contact on whites is a deliberate technical departure that sets his house style apart from the richer, more oak-influenced Meursault tradition.

parent_region: Nuits-Saint-Georges, Côte de Nuits, Burgundy, France; additional holdings in Vosne-Romanée, Gevrey-Chambertin, Chambolle-Musigny, Aloxe-Corton, and Moulin-à-Vent (Beaujolais)
description_short_en: Thibault Liger-Belair is the first vigneron in nine generations of a family that was exclusively a négociant — the Liger-Belairs once owned La Tâche Grand Cru (sold to DRC in 1929) and were listed on the Lyon Stock Exchange. His flagship…
description_full_en: Founded 2001 by Thibault Liger-Belair (family wine lineage traces to 1720 via C.Marey trading house, Nuits-Saint-Georges). Owner: Thibault Liger-Belair (independent, family-owned). Region: Nuits-Saint-Georges, Côte de Nuits, Burgundy, France; additional holdings in Vosne-Romanée, Gevrey-Chambertin, Chambolle-Musigny, Aloxe-Corton, and Moulin-à-Vent (Beaujolais). Classification: Multiple appellations: Richebourg Grand Cru (AOC), Clos de Vougeot Grand Cru (AOC), Nuits-Saint-Georges Premier Cru Les Saint-Georges (AOC), Aloxe-Corton Premier Cru La Toppe au Vert (AOC), and village/regional Bourgogne AOC wines. Style: Thibault Liger-Belair produces wines of precision, finesse, and terroir transparency rather than extraction-driven power — a deliberate departure from the historically robust Nuits-Saint-Georges archetype. His philosophy of 'infusion over extraction,' combined with fully biodynamic viticulture and minimal cellar intervention, yields wines with silky tannins, vibrant acidity, and a quiet mineral intensity. The use of variable whole-cluster fermentation (calibrated per vintage and parcel) and lightly charred, fine-grained oak adds aromatic complexity without masking fruit purity. Site: Approximately 15 hectares across the Côte de Nuits and Côte de Beaune. Key sites: Les Saint-Georges (2.05 ha, planted 1944, southern Nuits-Saint-Georges, clay-limestone soils with dense white pebbles, one of Burgundy's oldest-planted climates — vines documented since year 1000); Richebourg Grand Cru (~0.60 ha, Vosne-Romanée); Clos de Vougeot Grand Cru (0.73 ha, planted 1948, southern sector near Échézeaux wall); La Charmotte village NSG (30–40 cm clay over limestone, north of Nuits). Vineyards are plowed by horse; soils are limestone and chaille (limestone-flint mix) with clay. Moulin-à-Vent holdings (~8 ha) on granite soils. Vinification: Hand-harvested grapes with triple sorting (at vine, before destemmer, after destemmer). Native/indigenous yeast fermentation in open-top vessels. Variable whole-cluster inclusion depending on vintage and appellation level — Grand Crus receive higher whole-bunch percentages (up to ~40% documented for Les Saint-Georges 2018); village wines typically lower. Minimal pumpovers and pigeage; philosophy is gentle infusion rather than extraction. Aging: Oak barrels, 14–18 months depending on appellation, without racking or aeration. Maximum 50% new oak; Thibault selects oak species and grain per individual wine after fermentation, imposing a 3-year wood-drying period for silkier tannin integration. Lightly charred (5–7 minutes). Village wines typically one-third new, two-thirds older barrels. Bottling: Unfined and unfiltered. ABV not specified in sources (standard Burgundy range ~13–13.5%). Sulfites sourced from a natural mine in Poland. Blend/recipe: 100% Pinot Noir for all red Burgundy cuvées. White wines include Bourgogne Aligoté (Clos des Perrières). No blending across appellations except the entry-level 'Les Deux Terres' Bourgogne, which blends Côte d'Or and Beaujolais fruit. Moulin-à-Vent wines are 100% Gamay. Must know: Thibault Liger-Belair is the first vigneron in nine generations of a family that was exclusively a négociant — the Liger-Belairs once owned La Tâche Grand Cru (sold to DRC in 1929) and were listed on the Lyon Stock Exchange. His flagship Les Saint-Georges Premier Cru is at the centre of an ongoing campaign (since 2007) to have the climat reclassified as a Grand Cru, a promotion widely supported by critics who regard it as 'the Grand Cru Nuits never received.'

parent_region: Speyside, Ballindalloch, Banffshire, Scotland (at the foot of Ben Rinnes, ~800 ft elevation)
description_short_en: Glenfarclas pioneered the commercial cask-strength single malt category in 1968 with the Glenfarclas 105 — widely regarded as the world's first regularly available cask-strength bottling — and is one of the very few Scottish distilleries…
description_full_en: Founded 1836 by Robert Hay (licensed); purchased and continuously family-operated from 1865 by John Grant. Owner: J & G Grant (Grant family, independent — sixth generation as of 2026). Region: Speyside, Ballindalloch, Banffshire, Scotland (at the foot of Ben Rinnes, ~800 ft elevation). Classification: Single Malt Scotch Whisky, Speyside (labelled 'Single Highland Malt' on older bottlings, reflecting the historical inclusion of Speyside within the Highland region). Style: Glenfarclas is defined by an unwavering commitment to Spanish Oloroso sherry cask maturation, producing a full-bodied, richly fruited style — dried fruit, fruitcake, roasted nuts, warm spice, and dark chocolate — that is deeper and more structured than most Speyside peers. The use of the largest direct gas-fired copper pot stills in Speyside generates Maillard-reaction-driven caramelisation and a robust, oily new make that integrates exceptionally well with long sherry maturation. Unlike many competitors, Glenfarclas releases no NAS expressions in its core range, maintaining a full ladder of age-stated bottlings from 10 to 40 years old. Site: Rechlerich Farm site, Ballindalloch, Banffshire AB37 9BD. Situated on the north-western slopes of Ben Rinnes (~800 ft / ~244 m elevation). Process water drawn from the springs of Ben Rinnes — soft and slightly mineral. Unpeated malted barley sourced from commercial maltsters (primarily Scottish/Moray-grown) since the 1970s, stored in eleven on-site silos totalling ~3,600 tonnes capacity. Approximately 68,000 casks maturing on site in traditional dunnage warehouses (earth floors, stone walls, three stacks high), with stock from every year from 1953 to present. Vinification: Mash tun: steel lauter-style, 10 m diameter, 16.5-tonne grist capacity — one of the largest in Scotland. Three mash waters: first at 64°C, second at 78°C, third at 80°C (third water recycled as first water for next mash). Wort cooled to 20°C before transfer to twelve closed stainless-steel washbacks of ~45,000 litres each. Fermentation with cultured distillers' yeast at ~1% ratio; minimum 60 hours, typically averaging ~106 hours, yielding wash at ~8% ABV. Six copper pot stills — three wash stills (~29,600 L each) and three spirit stills (~25,000 L each) — all heated by direct gas flame (direct firing abandoned briefly with steam coils in the early 1980s but reverted within weeks due to loss of spirit depth and character). Wash stills fitted with copper chain rummagers to prevent scorching. Slow distillation runs with generous reflux via large boil balls on still necks. Spirit cut at ~68% ABV (~4,000 L per run). Shell-and-tube condensers. Aging: Primarily Spanish Oloroso sherry butts (ex-Oloroso, sourced exclusively from José Miguel Martín); casks reused up to four times (first-fill through fourth-fill). The 15 YO uses ~60% first- and second-fill Oloroso butts with ~40% third- and fourth-fill. Some expressions (particularly older Family Casks and certain limited releases) also use ex-bourbon barrels or PX hogsheads. New spirit filled into casks at 63.5% ABV. Matured in traditional dunnage warehouses on site. Age range: 10, 12, 15, 17, 21, 25, 30, 40 years (core range); Family Casks extend to single vintages from 1952 onward. Bottling: Always at natural colour (no caramel added). No chill-filtration on the 15 YO (46% ABV) and higher-strength expressions. Core range bottled at 40% (10 YO), 43% (12, 17, 21, 25, 30, 40 YO), and 46% (15 YO). Glenfarclas 105 bottled at a fixed 60% ABV (105 old British proof) — a vatted cask-strength expression, not a single cask. Bottling carried out at Broxburn Bottlers near Edinburgh. Blend/recipe: 100% unpeated malted barley (single malt). No grain bill variation. Cask recipe varies by expression: core range dominated by ex-Oloroso sherry butts (Spanish oak, European oak); the 15 YO is approximately 60% first/second-fill and 40% third/fourth-fill Oloroso. The 105 is a multi-cask vatting held at exactly 60% ABV. Family Casks are single-cask, single-vintage bottlings. Must know: Glenfarclas pioneered the commercial cask-strength single malt category in 1968 with the Glenfarclas 105 — widely regarded as the world's first regularly available cask-strength bottling — and is one of the very few Scottish distilleries still using direct gas-fired pot stills, a method it briefly abandoned in the early 1980s before immediately reverting after finding steam heating fundamentally altered the spirit's character. As one of only three 19th-century single-malt distilleries remaining in continuous family ownership, it is also uniquely positioned to offer single-cask Family Cask bottlings from every vintage year back to 1952, an inventory depth no corporate-owned distillery can match.

description_short_en: Glenfiddich is credited with single-handedly creating the modern global single malt category: in 1963 it became the first Scotch distillery to actively market and export single malt as a premium product in its own right, at a time when…
description_full_en: Founded 1886 by William Grant (distillery construction began 1886; first spirit ran Christmas Day 1887). Owner: William Grant & Sons Ltd. (privately held; fifth generation of the Grant-Gordon family as of 2024). Region: Speyside Single Malt Scotch Whisky, Dufftown, Moray, Scotland. Classification: Single Malt Scotch Whisky (Speyside); no official cru classification applicable. Style: Glenfiddich is defined by a light, fresh, orchard-fruit character — notably green pear, candied apple, and vanilla — derived from small, uniquely shaped swan-neck copper pot stills and predominantly ex-bourbon American oak maturation. Unlike heavier, more sherried Speyside peers, the house style prioritises delicacy and approachability, with complexity added through expression-specific cask finishing and the proprietary Solera Vat system used for the 15 Year Old. Across all expressions, a marrying step in large oak tuns is mandatory, lending integration and roundness that distinguishes Glenfiddich from distilleries that bottle directly from individual casks. Site: Glenfiddich Distillery, Dufftown, Keith AB55 4DH, Moray, Scotland. Situated at the base of the Conval Hills in the Glen of the River Fiddich. Sole water source: the Robbie Dhu Spring, a mineral-rich highland spring in the Conval Hills — the Grant family purchased over 1,200 acres (some sources say 12,000 acres) of surrounding land specifically to protect this catchment. The valley has its own distinct microclimate. Barley is sourced preferentially from local Speyside farms exploiting the rich alluvial soils of the area. Stone-walled dunnage warehouses with earthen floors provide stable maturation conditions; on-site warehousing holds up to 800,000 casks. Vinification: 100% malted barley mash; grist milled to a ratio of approximately 10 parts flour to 20 parts husk for optimal drainage. Mash tuns process approximately 9 tonnes of grist per charge with Robbie Dhu spring water at three sequential temperatures to maximise starch-to-sugar conversion. Wort fermented in large wooden washbacks (traditionally Oregon pine) for approximately 72 hours. Double pot-still distillation in 43 (per Wikipedia; other sources cite 42) distinctively small, swan-neck copper pot stills — 14 pear-shaped wash stills (~9,000 L each) and 28 spirit stills (~4,500 L each) in two shapes: constricted-neck (old stillhouse, direct gas-fired) and reflux-bowl (new stillhouse, steam-heated, operational 2020). The two spirit-still cuts are blended to achieve Glenfiddich's characteristic light, fruity new make. Glenfiddich employs in-house coppersmiths to hand-build and maintain all stills, ensuring shape consistency across generations. Aging: Primarily ex-bourbon American white oak casks (small format); supplemented by ex-Oloroso sherry European oak casks. Expression-specific regimes: 12 YO — ex-bourbon (approx. 85%) and ex-sherry (approx. 15%) for minimum 12 years, then married in 2,000 L oak tuns for 6 months; 15 YO — virgin American oak, ex-bourbon, and ex-sherry for minimum 15 years, then married in the proprietary Solera Vat (Oregon pine, never more than half-emptied since 1998); 18 YO — includes 18–20% ex-Oloroso sherry cask component; 21 YO Gran Reserva — ex-bourbon for 21 years, finished 4 months in Caribbean rum casks; 23 YO Grand Cru — American and European oak, finished 6 months in French cuvée wine casks; 26 YO Grande Couronne — 2-year finish in French ex-cognac casks; 29 YO Grand Yozakura — finished 6 months in Japanese Awamori casks (world first); 31 YO Grand Château — 9-year finish in Bordeaux wine casks. All expressions undergo a marrying/vatting step before bottling. Bottling: Distilled, matured, and bottled entirely on-site at Dufftown (one of very few Highland distilleries to do so end-to-end); also a large bottling plant in Bellshill. Reduction to bottling strength uses Robbie Dhu spring water. Core range bottled at 40% ABV (standard). Cask-strength and non-chill-filtered releases exist (e.g., Experimental Series Project XX is non-chill-filtered). Core expressions are chill-filtered at standard ABV. Blend/recipe: 100% malted barley (single malt). No grain bill or blending with other distilleries. Cask split for 12 YO: approximately 85% ex-bourbon American oak / 15% ex-Oloroso sherry European oak. 15 YO Solera: three-way marriage of virgin American oak, ex-bourbon, and ex-sherry stocks in a never-emptied Solera Vat. Higher expressions use various finishing casks (rum, cognac, wine, Awamori) layered over a predominantly ex-bourbon base. Must know: Glenfiddich is credited with single-handedly creating the modern global single malt category: in 1963 it became the first Scotch distillery to actively market and export single malt as a premium product in its own right, at a time when blended Scotch held a near-monopoly. The 15 Year Old's Solera Vat — a large Oregon pine tun that has never been fully emptied since 1998 — is a genuine production innovation borrowed from sherry bodegas and means every bottle contains a trace of the original 1998 fill, giving the expression a continuity and complexity unmatched in the core Speyside category.

description_short_en: Glenturret claims to be Scotland's oldest working distillery (site documented to 1763, illicit use from 1717), though this is contested by other distilleries. Under Lalique Group ownership since 2019, it has pivoted entirely away from its…
description_full_en: Founded 1763 (as Thurot Distillery, evidenced by a rental document from Sir Patrick Murray of Ochtertyre); licensed under the Drummond family in 1818; renamed Glenturret in 1875 by Thomas Stewart. Modern revival by James Fairlie in 1957.. Owner: Lalique Group (led by Silvio Denz) and Swiss philanthropist Hansjörg Wyss — joint venture 'Glenturret Holding', acquired March 2019. Region: Highland Single Malt Scotch Whisky, Crieff, Perthshire, Scotland (parish of Monzievaird and Strowan). Classification: Highland Single Malt Scotch Whisky (no grand cru or formal classification system applies). Style: Glenturret produces a distinctively light, fruity, and acidic new-make spirit from one of Scotland's smallest still pairs, with the house style now firmly anchored in unpeated malt matured predominantly in American oak sherry-seasoned casks, yielding notes of tropical fruit, honey, vanilla, and spice. Unlike many Highland peers that lean on heavily sherried or smoky profiles, Glenturret's post-2019 Lalique-era direction emphasises elegance and terroir-driven delicacy over weight. The Triple Wood expression layers American oak ex-bourbon and both American and European oak sherry-seasoned casks for added complexity. Site: Located in the 'Hosh' glen on the banks of the Turret Burn (a tributary of the River Tay), approximately 2 miles northwest of Crieff, Perthshire. Process water is sourced exclusively via a dedicated pipeline from Loch Turret, a freshwater reservoir in the Grampian Mountains fed by runoff from Ben Chonzie; the geology of the catchment produces exceptionally soft water. The distillery sits in a sheltered, high-sided glen at low elevation; the secluded microclimate historically attracted illicit distillers. Six on-site dunnage warehouses hold up to 10,000 casks. Vinification: Barley (sourced exclusively from Scotland) is soaked in Loch Turret water for 2–3 days, then floor-malted and kiln-dried (historically over peat smoke; from 2025 fully unpeated). Mashing in a small mash tun (~1 tonne of grist) at ~70°C with three waters. Fermentation in traditional wooden (pine) washbacks for approximately 48 hours. Distillation in a single wash still (12,500 litres, distinctive cone shape) and a single spirit still (9,000 litres, fitted with a reflux bowl for additional copper contact and smoothness); both traditional copper pot stills. Only the middle cut ('Heart of the Run') is retained. Aging: Predominantly American oak sherry-seasoned casks; also European oak sherry-seasoned casks and ex-bourbon barrels used in multi-wood expressions. Minimum 3 years for blending stock; core single malt range matured 7, 10, 12, 15, and 21+ years. Six on-site dunnage warehouses. New/used wood split not publicly specified. Bottling: Bottled at varying ABVs (may shift slightly year-on-year by release); not consistently cask strength for core range. Filtration and fining status not publicly confirmed for all expressions. Blend/recipe: 100% Scottish malted barley (single malt); unpeated from 2025 onward. No grain bill or specific barley variety publicly stated. Core range includes Triple Wood (American oak ex-bourbon + American and European oak sherry-seasoned casks), and age-stated expressions (7, 10, 12, 15, 21 YO). Must know: Glenturret claims to be Scotland's oldest working distillery (site documented to 1763, illicit use from 1717), though this is contested by other distilleries. Under Lalique Group ownership since 2019, it has pivoted entirely away from its former role as the primary malt component of The Famous Grouse blend, now producing exclusively for its own single malt range — a dramatic identity shift — and hosts the only Michelin two-starred restaurant located within a Scotch whisky distillery.

description_short_en: Hennessy invented two of the cognac industry's most enduring classification standards: the VS star-rating system (created by Maurice Hennessy, later adopted industry-wide) and the XO category itself (created by Maurice Hennessy in 1870,…
description_full_en: Founded 1765 by Richard Hennessy. Owner: Moët Hennessy (LVMH 66%, Diageo 34%). Region: Cognac AOC, Charente & Charente-Maritime, France — sourcing primarily from Grande Champagne, Petite Champagne, Borderies, and Fins Bois crus. Classification: Cognac AOC (Appellation d'Origine Contrôlée). Multi-cru blended house; not a single-cru designation. Grading tiers: VS (min. 2 yrs), VSOP (min. 4 yrs), XO (min. 10 yrs), Paradis, Richard Hennessy.. Style: Hennessy is defined by a bold, full-bodied, and assertive house style — richer and more powerful than Martell (Borderies-focused, lighter) or Rémy Martin (Fine Champagne, more floral-spicy). This power comes partly from distilling both with and without lees, and from adding heads and tails back to the brouillis in the second distillation, concentrating congeners for greater depth. The result is a cognac profile of dark fruit, oak, vanilla, and rising spice, with a long, warming finish. Site: Headquartered on the banks of the Charente River in the town of Cognac, France. Hennessy owns approximately 180–200 hectares of vineyards used primarily for R&D and viticultural research. The vast majority of grapes are sourced from over 1,500–1,600 independent partner growers across the top four crus (Grande Champagne, Petite Champagne, Borderies, Fins Bois). Soils range from the shallow clay-limestone chalk of Grande Champagne (ideal for long aging) to the clay-flint of Borderies. Eaux-de-vie are aged in 65 cellars (chais); those near the humid Charente riverbank yield mellower, rounder spirits, while drier inland chais produce more characterful eaux-de-vie. Hennessy holds approximately 350,000–470,000 casks — the world's largest cognac reserve. Vinification: Ugni Blanc grapes (>95% of production) are harvested in September–October, pressed, and fermented with native wild yeast for approximately 5–7 days with no added sugar or sulphur, producing a low-alcohol (8–10% ABV), high-acidity base wine. Double distillation is performed in traditional Charentais copper pot stills (alembic charentais); the first pass produces 'brouillis', the second concentrates to ~70% ABV, retaining only the 'heart' (coeur). Hennessy distills both with and without lees (unlike Martell, which never uses lees), and adds heads and tails back to the brouillis for the second distillation, yielding a richer, deeper spirit. Distillation is conducted by 19 dedicated distilleries and must be completed before 31 March each year. Each batch takes approximately 24 hours. Aging: Eaux-de-vie are aged in French oak barrels (Limousin oak), including new barrels for initial aging (e.g., VS is matured in new oak). Hennessy operates its own cooperage in Cognac, producing 400–500 new barrels per year by hand, and sources additional staves from its own Limousin forests (purchased ~42 years ago; trees must be 80+ years old before felling). Eaux-de-vie are rotated between different barrels and cellars over years to decades. The Tasting Committee reviews ~10,000 eaux-de-vie annually. XO blends eaux-de-vie aged up to ~30 years; Richard Hennessy uses eaux-de-vie aged at least 40 years. Bottling: Blended to standard ABV (typically 40% for most expressions, as per Cognac AOC minimum). Lightly filtered before bottling. Not cask strength for core range. Limited and prestige expressions (Paradis Impérial, Richard Hennessy) are bottled in hand-blown crystal decanters. Blend/recipe: Ugni Blanc dominant (>95% of production; occasionally trace Colombard or Folle Blanche permitted under AOC). Multi-cru blend drawing primarily from Grande Champagne, Petite Champagne, Borderies, and Fins Bois. XO: blend of 100+ eaux-de-vie aged up to ~30 years. Paradis: blend of 100+ exceptional eaux-de-vie, of which only ~10 per 10,000 assessed annually qualify. Richard Hennessy: ultra-rare blend aged 40+ years. Must know: Hennessy invented two of the cognac industry's most enduring classification standards: the VS star-rating system (created by Maurice Hennessy, later adopted industry-wide) and the XO category itself (created by Maurice Hennessy in 1870, originally for family and friends). The Fillioux family has served as Hennessy's sole Master Blender lineage for eight consecutive generations — an unbroken 200+ year partnership unique in the global spirits industry.

parent_region: Eden Valley GI, Barossa Zone, South Australia (also Barossa Valley and Adelaide Hills/Lenswood)
description_short_en: Cyril Henschke (4th generation) is credited with producing Australia's first single-vineyard wines — Mount Edelstone Shiraz from 1952 and Hill of Grace Shiraz from 1958 — at a time when blended and fortified wines were the Australian norm,…
description_full_en: Founded 1868 by Johann Christian Henschke (vines first planted c.1862). Owner: Henschke family (independent; sixth generation — Stephen & Prue Henschke, with children Johann, Justine, and Andreas). Region: Eden Valley GI, Barossa Zone, South Australia (also Barossa Valley and Adelaide Hills/Lenswood). Classification: No formal classification system applies in Australia. Henschke is a member of Australia's First Families of Wine. Hill of Grace and Mount Edelstone are widely regarded as Australia's benchmark single-vineyard Shiraz wines.. Style: Henschke's house style is defined by terroir-driven, single-vineyard Shiraz of extraordinary old-vine intensity married to cool-climate Eden Valley elegance — a combination of dark fruit concentration, five-spice/star-anise aromatics, fine-grained tannins, and natural acidity that sets it apart from the richer, more opulent floor-Barossa style. Winemaker Stephen Henschke avoids tannin additions and relies on natural grape acidity, while viticulturist Prue Henschke's biodynamic and organic farming has progressively refined fruit quality and tannin maturity over four decades. The result is wines of uncommon longevity and precision that age gracefully for 30–50+ years. Site: Primary sites: (1) Hill of Grace Vineyard — 8 ha (4 ha old-vine Shiraz contributing to the wine), Parrot Hill, 4 km NW of Henschke Cellars; elevation 400 m; alluvial sandy loam over red-brown earth grading to deep silty loam with patches of limestone and schist; average rainfall 520 mm; dry-grown, own-rooted, ungrafted vines; oldest 'Grandfather' block planted c.1860 by Nicolaus Stanitzki; named after the adjacent Gnadenberg Lutheran Church. (2) Mount Edelstone Vineyard — 16 ha, planted 1912 from James Busby vine stock, own-rooted pre-phylloxera; elevation 400 m, easterly aspect, Scott Henry trellis. (3) Eden Valley Vineyard — Cranes Range Road, ~500 m elevation, sandy loam over sandstone/schist; oldest vines 1968. (4) Lenswood Vineyard — 13 ha, Adelaide Hills, planted mid-1980s. All vineyards farmed organically and biodynamically. Vinification: Hand-harvested grapes fermented in traditional open-top concrete fermenters (Hill of Grace) or open-top vessels with submerged-cap technique; full malolactic fermentation; no added tannins; natural acidity relied upon where possible from old vines; optical sorting introduced from 2024 vintage. Aging: Hill of Grace: 18 months in French and American oak hogsheads — recent releases approximately 86% French / 14% American, ~33% new / 67% seasoned (2021 vintage saw no new oak for the first time). Mount Edelstone: similar hogshead regime. Separate block parcels blended prior to bottling. Bottling: All Henschke wines bottled under Stelvin screwcap from the 2021 single-vineyard release onward (previously Vinolok glass stopper; screwcap adopted for entry-level wines from c.2001). ABV typically 14–14.5%. Fining agent: vegan. Filtration status not publicly specified. Blend/recipe: Hill of Grace: 100% Shiraz from six blocks within the 8-ha vineyard (oldest 'Grandfather' block c.0.56 ha planted c.1860; other blocks 1910, 1951, 1952, 1956, 1965). Mount Edelstone: 100% Shiraz, 16-ha single vineyard planted 1912. Keyneton Euphonium: Shiraz, Cabernet Sauvignon, Cabernet Franc, and Merlot blend. Julius Riesling: 100% Riesling, Eden Valley. Cyril Henschke: 100% Cabernet Sauvignon, Eden Valley. Must know: Cyril Henschke (4th generation) is credited with producing Australia's first single-vineyard wines — Mount Edelstone Shiraz from 1952 and Hill of Grace Shiraz from 1958 — at a time when blended and fortified wines were the Australian norm, pioneering a category that now defines the country's fine-wine identity. Uniquely, the Hill of Grace 'Grandfather' vines (planted c.1860) are own-rooted and pre-phylloxera, surviving on their original rootstock for over 160 years; the vineyard is technically owned by a separate branch of the Henschke family (descendants of Louis Edmund Henschke), with Stephen and Prue operating under a sharecropping agreement.

parent_region: Kirkwall, Orkney Islands, Scotland (Island single malt; Orkney is not a legally defined SWA region but is classified under 'Islands')
description_short_en: Highland Park is one of only a handful of Scotch distilleries still conducting traditional floor maltings on-site, and the only distillery in the world to use Orkney heather-peat from Hobbister Moor — a peat type so distinct (no tree…
description_full_en: Founded 1798 by Magnus Eunson (illicit distiller) / David Robertson (first licensed operator) — sources conflict; official licence granted 1826. Owner: The Edrington Group (acquired 1999 when Edrington purchased Highland Distillers plc). Region: Kirkwall, Orkney Islands, Scotland (Island single malt; Orkney is not a legally defined SWA region but is classified under 'Islands'). Classification: Single Malt Scotch Whisky (Island / Orkney). No formal cru classification. Scotch Whisky Regulations compliant.. Style: Highland Park is defined by the unique interplay of aromatic Orcadian heather-peat smoke (soft and floral, never medicinal or tarry like Islay) and rich sherry-oak sweetness from Oloroso-seasoned European and American oak casks. The result is a house style of heather honey, dried fruit, gentle smoke, and maritime salinity that sits between Speyside's fruit-forward richness and Islay's peaty intensity. Whisky writer Michael Jackson famously called it 'the greatest all-rounder in the world of malt whisky.' Site: Highland Park Distillery, Holm Road, Kirkwall, Orkney KW15 1SU. Sits on a hillside on Mainland Orkney at low elevation, exposed to North Sea and Atlantic maritime climate — cool, temperate, and consistent year-round, ideal for slow maturation. Peat is hand-cut from Hobbister Moor, ~7 miles southwest of the distillery; Orkney peat is shallow, heather-rich, and tree-root-free, producing aromatic floral smoke at ~20 ppm phenol. Water drawn from Cattie Maggie's Spring (also referenced as Crantit Spring in some sources) — notably hard mineral water, unusual among Scotch distilleries. 23 stone warehouses on-site (19 traditional dunnage with earthen floors, remainder racked), providing stable cool maturation conditions. Vinification: Roughly 20% of annual malt requirement is floor-malted on-site using traditional hand-turning; kilned with Hobbister Moor heather-peat to ~20 ppm phenol. Remaining ~80% is unpeated malt sourced from the Scottish mainland (Black Isle). Mashing in a stainless-steel full-lauter mash tun (11–11.4 tonnes). Fermentation in 12 Oregon pine (Douglas fir) washbacks of 29,200 litres each; fermentation time 50–80 hours depending on season, using Kerry M & MX yeast strains, producing wash at ~8% ABV. Double distillation in 2 wash stills and 2 spirit stills (pear-shaped with spherical lids and conical necks, steam-heated); low reflux design yields a medium-heavy new make spirit characteristic of Island whiskies. Aging: Matured in sherry-seasoned European oak (Oloroso) and American oak casks, both first-fill and refill, purchased from Oloroso sherry producers in Spain. Since ~2004, policy shifted to exclusive use of ex-sherry casks (both European and American oak). A cask harmonisation / marrying period is employed before bottling to integrate flavours. Age statements range from 10 to 50 years in the core range. Maturation on-site in dunnage and racked warehouses in Orkney's temperate maritime climate. Bottling: Core expressions bottled at 40–46% ABV (e.g. 12 YO at 40%, 18 YO at 43%, 21 YO at 46%). Cask Strength releases bottled without dilution (e.g. ~63.9% ABV). Natural colour (no caramel addition stated on premium expressions). Chill-filtration status varies by expression; higher-ABV and premium releases are typically non-chill-filtered. Blend/recipe: 100% malted barley (single malt). Approximately 20% peated floor-malted barley from on-site maltings (~20 ppm phenol, Hobbister Moor heather-peat) blended with ~80% unpeated malt from mainland Scotland. No grain whisky. Vatted from multiple casks of varying ages and cask types (first-fill and refill European and American oak sherry-seasoned casks) before bottling. Must know: Highland Park is one of only a handful of Scotch distilleries still conducting traditional floor maltings on-site, and the only distillery in the world to use Orkney heather-peat from Hobbister Moor — a peat type so distinct (no tree roots, rich in heather and fern) that trials with mainland peated malt produced a noticeably different whisky. Its 25 Year Old was the first spirit ever to receive a perfect 100-point score at the Ultimate Spirits Challenge, and the distillery has been named 'Best Spirit in the World' three times by F. Paul Pacult.

description_short_en: Hine is the only Cognac house to hold a British Royal Warrant and one of only three houses (alongside Delamain and Croizet) to have continuously maintained stocks of vintage single-harvest Cognacs — with library stocks dating to 1850. Its…
description_full_en: Founded 1763; Thomas Hine (born Dorset, England) arrived in Jarnac in 1791, married into the founding négociant family, and renamed the house Thomas Hine & Co. in 1817. Owner: EDV SAS (French company; acquired 2013). Region: Jarnac, Charente — sourcing exclusively from Grande Champagne and Petite Champagne crus, Cognac AOC. Classification: Cognac AOC (Fine Champagne and Grande Champagne designations depending on expression); only Cognac house holding a UK Royal Warrant (granted 1962 by Queen Elizabeth II, renewed by King Charles III). Style: Hine pursues a house philosophy that great Cognac is above all a great white wine — prioritising delicacy, vivacity, and floral-fruit finesse over power or heavy oak. Unlike the 'Big Four', Hine is one of very few houses to maintain two distinct ageing regimes: Jarnac-matured (richer, more structured, rancio-driven) and 'early-landed' (aged in Bristol, England, yielding lighter, fresher, more floral expressions). The house is also a rare commercial pioneer of single-vintage and single-estate Cognacs. Site: Domaine de Bonneuil, village of Bonneuil, Grande Champagne cru: 115 hectares (approx. 173 acres per some sources — see uncertainty flags) of Ugni Blanc vines on rolling limestone-chalk valleys. Vines are kept young (20–25 years) to preserve acidity and yield. The Chai des Millésimes cellar in Jarnac sits 2 metres below the level of the River Charente, with humidity varying 70–90% and temperatures ranging 6.5°C (winter) to 20.5°C (summer). Early-landed casks are held in Bristol cellars at near-constant low temperature and humidity rarely below 95%. Vinification: Ugni Blanc grapes harvested and fermented in large temperature-controlled vats for 7–9 days using native wild yeast (no sulphites permitted under AOC rules), producing a dry base wine of ~7–8% ABV. Double distillation in traditional Charentais copper alembic pot stills, conducted on the fine lees (sur lies) — lees distillation is a deliberate Hine signature that adds pastry notes and roundness. Aging: Initial months in new/lightly toasted French oak (fine-grained), then transferred to older seasoned oak casks to moderate tannin extraction. Ultra-aged vintage casks are rebarrelled into refill casks at least twice over decades to control oxidation and oak contribution. Angels' share ~2–3% per year. Early-landed vintages are shipped to Bristol after a minimum 2 years in Jarnac (regulatory requirement) and aged there for up to 20 years before return to Jarnac for bottling. XO 1er Cru aged over a decade in French oak. Bottling: Standard expressions bottled at 40% ABV with dilution added gradually over years ('breaking down the blend'). Single-cask and vintage releases may be bottled at cask strength (e.g. 1975 50-year-old at 47% ABV). Filtration specifics not publicly confirmed. Blend/recipe: 100% Ugni Blanc. Core blends (e.g. Rare VSOP Fine Champagne) draw from both Grande Champagne and Petite Champagne eaux-de-vie aged 6–12 years. XO 1er Cru is 100% Grande Champagne. H by Hine VSOP blends ~20 eaux-de-vie from Grande and Petite Champagne, minimum 4 years aged. Single-estate Bonneuil is 100% Grande Champagne, single-vintage, unblended. Must know: Hine is the only Cognac house to hold a British Royal Warrant and one of only three houses (alongside Delamain and Croizet) to have continuously maintained stocks of vintage single-harvest Cognacs — with library stocks dating to 1850. Its 'early-landed, late-bottled' practice — shipping casks to Bristol for extended UK maturation — produces a demonstrably different style from the same base vintage, making side-by-side comparisons a unique educational exercise unavailable at any other major house.

parent_region: Blended Scotch Whisky — sourced from distilleries across all major Scottish regions: Speyside, Highland, Islay, and Lowland; blended and bottled at Diageo facilities in Leven, Fife, and Shieldhall, near Glasgow
description_short_en: Johnnie Walker pioneered the modern concept of consistent, branded blended Scotch at commercial scale — John Walker's original insight (borrowed from his tea-blending experience) was that blending across regions and seasons could eliminate…
description_full_en: Founded 1820 by John Walker (born 25 July 1805, Kilmarnock, Ayrshire, Scotland; opened grocery and spirits shop in Kilmarnock in 1820). Owner: Diageo plc (London-based publicly traded multinational; acquired via Distillers Company 1925 → Guinness 1986 → Diageo merger with Grand Metropolitan 1997). Region: Blended Scotch Whisky — sourced from distilleries across all major Scottish regions: Speyside, Highland, Islay, and Lowland; blended and bottled at Diageo facilities in Leven, Fife, and Shieldhall, near Glasgow. Classification: Blended Scotch Whisky (core range); Blended Malt Scotch Whisky (Green Label, 15 YO); no single-distillery or appellation classification applies — regulated under Scotch Whisky Regulations 2009. Style: Johnnie Walker's defining house character is a deliberate pan-Scotland blend architecture: Cardhu (Speyside) provides a honeyed, fruit-forward sweetness as the 'heart'; Caol Ila (Islay) delivers clean maritime peat smoke; and Clynelish (Highland) adds waxy, coastal depth — a combination that no single-region producer can replicate. The brand's master blenders (currently Dr. Emma Walker, the first woman to hold the role in the brand's 200-year history) work from a reserve of approximately 10 million casks to maintain absolute batch-to-batch consistency across millions of bottles globally. Unlike most blended Scotch houses that lean toward either grain-heavy lightness or malt-forward richness, Johnnie Walker deliberately threads smoke, sweetness, and spice in equal measure across its entire tiered range. Site: Not a single distillery — Johnnie Walker is a blending house drawing from a network of Diageo-owned malt and grain distilleries across Scotland. Key malt contributors: Cardhu (Speyside, 'heart' of the blend, acquired by the Walker family in 1893); Caol Ila (Islay, maritime peat); Clynelish (Highland, waxy depth); Talisker (Isle of Skye, intense smoke and spice, prominent in Green Label); Linkwood and Cragganmore (Speyside, fragrance and maltiness); Glenkinchie (Lowland, light floral). Grain whisky is produced at Diageo's column-still grain distilleries. Blending and bottling operations are based at Leven, Fife, and Shieldhall, Glasgow (the original Kilmarnock Hill Street plant — once the world's largest bottling facility — closed March 2012). Vinification: Malt whiskies: malted barley steeped in water, spread on malting floor to germinate, then kiln-dried (peat optionally burned during drying to impart smokiness); mash tun extraction of fermentable sugars; fermentation with yeast produces a wash (beer-like liquid); double distillation in copper pot stills (for malt component). Grain whiskies: continuous column (Coffey) still distillation from wheat or maize with a proportion of malted barley. Each cask is individually analyzed and approved by the blending team before selection. Aging: Matured in oak casks (predominantly ex-bourbon American oak barrels and ex-Sherry European oak casks) for a minimum of 3 years by law; expression-specific minimums: Red Label — no age statement (NAS); Black Label — minimum 12 years; Green Label — minimum 15 years (blended malt only); Gold Label Reserve — NAS premium; Blue Label — NAS, hand-selected rare casks (only 1 in 10,000 casks qualify). Specific new-oak percentage and exact cask split by expression are not publicly disclosed. Bottling: Chill-filtered and reduced to bottling strength for most expressions; Blue Label bottled at 40% ABV; Black Label at 40% ABV; Green Label at 43% ABV. Cask-strength or unfiltered expressions exist only in ultra-limited John Walker & Sons luxury releases (e.g., Diamond Jubilee). Standard expressions are not bottled at cask strength. Blend/recipe: Red Label: over 30 malt and grain whiskies from across Scotland (rich east-coast malts + smoky west-coast malts). Black Label: approximately 40 malt and grain whiskies including Cardhu, Caol Ila, and Clynelish, all aged minimum 12 years. Green Label (blended malt, no grain): Talisker (Isle of Skye), Linkwood (Speyside), Cragganmore (Speyside), Caol Ila (Islay), all minimum 15 years — exact proportions undisclosed. Blue Label: rare, hand-selected casks from across Diageo's portfolio including occasional 'ghost distillery' whiskies (e.g., Brora, Port Ellen in Ghost & Rare series); exact recipe a trade secret. Grain whisky percentage by expression is not publicly disclosed. Must know: Johnnie Walker pioneered the modern concept of consistent, branded blended Scotch at commercial scale — John Walker's original insight (borrowed from his tea-blending experience) was that blending across regions and seasons could eliminate batch variation, a radical departure from the single-malt norm of his era. The brand's Blue Label 'Ghost & Rare' series is one of the only commercially available vehicles for tasting whisky from permanently closed 'ghost' distilleries such as Brora and Port Ellen, making it a historically significant collector's category beyond its luxury positioning.
