parent_region: Champagne AOC, Reims, Montagne de Reims / Côte des Blancs / Vallée de la Marne, France
description_short_en: Veuve Clicquot is the house that invented three pillars of modern Champagne: the first recorded single-vintage wine (1810), the riddling table/pupitre (1816, enabling mass clarification), and the first blended rosé Champagne (1818) — all…
description_full_en: Founded 1772 by Philippe Clicquot (a textile merchant and vineyard owner from Reims); the house was transformed and renamed under his daughter-in-law Barbe-Nicole Clicquot Ponsardin ('La Veuve') from 1805 onward. Owner: Moët Hennessy (Wines & Spirits division of LVMH — Louis Vuitton Moët Hennessy); acquired 1986–1987. Region: Champagne AOC, Reims, Montagne de Reims / Côte des Blancs / Vallée de la Marne, France. Classification: Champagne AOC (non-vintage and vintage Brut, Rosé, Demi-Sec, and prestige cuvée La Grande Dame); no single-vineyard classification applies — house style draws from 12 of 17 Grand Cru villages and 20 of 44 Premier Cru villages. Style: Veuve Clicquot is defined by a Pinot Noir-dominant, full-bodied house style that prizes strength, complexity, and generous fruit over delicate minerality — a deliberate contrast to Chardonnay-led houses such as Salon or Taittinger Comtes. The Yellow Label NV is built on 25–50% reserve wines from up to 50–60 crus, delivering consistent richness and toasty brioche character across every release. La Grande Dame pushes this philosophy to its extreme, with 90–92% Grand Cru Pinot Noir and a stated goal of 'verticality, elegance, and sharpness' rather than opulence. Site: 393 hectares of estate vineyards spread across 12 of 17 Grand Cru villages (including Bouzy, Ambonnay, Aÿ, Verzy, Verzenay, Avize, Le Mesnil-sur-Oger) and 20 of 44 Premier Cru villages; approximately 86% of vines classified Grand Cru or Premier Cru. Soils are shallow chalk (craie) on hillside slopes, providing maximum sun exposure and natural drainage. Cellars: 24 km of UNESCO World Heritage-listed chalk crayères (former quarries) beneath the Colline Saint-Nicaise, Reims, providing stable temperature and humidity for extended aging. Estate fruit covers ~20% of total production; the remaining 80% is sourced from ~400 long-term grower partners. Vinification: Hand-harvested grapes pressed in whole bunches at six dedicated pressing centres (29 presses total); each variety, fraction, and cru vinified separately. Alcoholic fermentation in stainless steel vats, 8–10 days. Full malolactic fermentation carried out across the range to soften acidity. Since 2008, a small proportion of Vintage and Yellow Label base wines are aged in large oak foudres for added complexity; La Grande Dame is 100% stainless steel. Roughly 700–800 individual base wine samples are assembled by Cellar Master Didier Mariotti (11th in the house's history). Aging: Yellow Label NV: minimum 30 months on lees in bottle (significantly above the 15-month AOC minimum); Vintage Brut: 5–10 years on lees; La Grande Dame: minimum 7 years on lees (2008 aged 9–10 years; 2012 aged 7 years; 2015 aged 7+ years). Reserve wines held 1–30 years. La Grande Dame riddled by hand; all other cuvées semi-automatic gyropalette riddling. Bottling: Chill-filtered; not cask strength. Dosage: Yellow Label NV 9 g/L; La Grande Dame 6 g/L; Extra Brut Extra Old 3 g/L; Demi-Sec ~45 g/L. ABV typically 12–12.5%. Disgorgement date and dosage printed on back label of La Grande Dame. Blend/recipe: Yellow Label NV: Pinot Noir dominant (approx. 50–55%), Chardonnay (28–33%), Pinot Meunier (15–20%); blended from 50–60 crus with 25–50% reserve wines. La Grande Dame: 90% Pinot Noir (Verzy, Bouzy, Verzenay, Aÿ, Ambonnay) + 10% Chardonnay (Avize, Le Mesnil-sur-Oger) — all Grand Cru; post-2008 formula. Rosé NV: 44–48% Pinot Noir, 25–29% Chardonnay, 13–18% Meunier, plus 12% still red wine (assemblage method, not saignée). Note: Wikipedia cites estate vineyard plantings as 50% Chardonnay / 45% Pinot Noir / 5% Meunier, while Millesima and Champagne Academy cite 47% Chardonnay / 36% Pinot Noir / 17% Meunier — figures likely reflect different measurement periods; conflict noted. Must know: Veuve Clicquot is the house that invented three pillars of modern Champagne: the first recorded single-vintage wine (1810), the riddling table/pupitre (1816, enabling mass clarification), and the first blended rosé Champagne (1818) — all under a 27-year-old widow at a time when women had no legal right to run a business. La Grande Dame is one of the very few prestige cuvées in Champagne with a Pinot Noir majority (90–92%), making it structurally closer to a blanc de noirs than to Cristal or Dom Pérignon, and it is released only in exceptional Pinot Noir years (2008, 2012, 2015, 2018 in the modern era).
